content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
(916) 322·5662 
Randal Friedman 
1360 Kelton #2 
Los Angeles, CA 
90024 
Dear Mr. Friedman: 
Administration 
•• 
Executive/legal 
•• 
Enforcement 
32?·5901 
322·6441 
September 23, 1983 
Statements. of Economic Interesf 
322·6444 
Re: 
Your Request for Written Advice 
Our No. A-83-2l4 
Your letter requesting our written advice pursuant to 
Government Code Section 83ll4(b)11 presents the following 
facts for our consideration. 
FACTS 
In the past, you were an employee of the California Coastal 
Commission (a state agency) and your employment terminated after 
January 1, 1981. 
You were employed by the Coastal Commission 
for three years. 
The first two years, you were on the Permit 
Appeals staff in the San Francisco office, during which time you 
handled several major permits in the Malibu coastal zone. 
The 
last year of your employment, you were assigned to the South 
Coast District and worked primarily on Malibu projects, 
including the Malibu Local Coastal Program. 
One of the cases 
which you worked on was the unsuccessful application of Murphy 
Dunne which came before the Coastal Commission for approval of a 
single-family residence. 
11 
The Political Reform Act (the "Act") consists of 
Government Code Sections 81000-91014, inclusive. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
specified. 


Randal Friedman 
September 23, 1983 
Page 2 
You have now been asked by State Senator Herschel Rosenthal 
to assist Murphy Dunne in a second (new) application for a 
single-family residence of a slightly different design than that 
submitted for his first application. 
Your involvement in this matter is at the request of the 
Senator. 
You are not under contract with Mr. Dunne, nor are you 
receiving any compensation from anyone for your work or 
appearance before the Coastal Commission. 
You have not been 
designated as Mr. Dunne's representative on the application for 
a coastal development permit. 
You have no expectation of any 
future compensation for this work. 
ANALYSIS 
You have asked us whether the above facts raise an issue of 
a conflict of interest on your part. 
Because you are no longer 
a public official, the conflict of interest provisions contained 
in Article 1 of Chapter 7 of the Act (Sections 87100-87103) do 
not apply. 
However, as a former state employee, the provisions 
of Article 4 of Chapter 7 (Sections 87400-87405) do apply. 
Generally, they provide that a former state employee may not 
represent, aid or assist, another party for compensation in a 
judicial or quasi-judicial or other proceeding involving the 
state, if the former employee had participated in the proceeding 
while with the state. 
Sections 87401 and 87402, which contain these prohibitions 
clearly require that the former employee be compensated for the 
representation or assistance which he or she provides. 
Since 
you will receive no compensation for your efforts, either now or 
in the future,ll the prohibitions do not apply to you. 
Article 4 of Chapter 7 of the Act was enacted to prohibit state 
employees from profiting from their "inside" knowledge of a 
particular proceeding gained while employed by the state. 
Volunteer activities are not precluded. 
CONCLUSION 
So long as you receive no compensation, from any source, for 
your participation in this proceeding you have no possibility 
for a conflict of interest under the Politial Reform Act. 
11 
This includes promises of future referrals of clients. 


Randal Friedman 
September 23, 1983 
Page 3 
Should you have further questions, please do not hesitate to 
call me at (916) 322-5901. 
Sincerely,

Robert E. 
digh 
Counsel 
~ 
I 
Legal Division 
REL:plh 


Barbara Millman, Chief 
Legal Division 
Fair Political Pratices Commission 
PO Box 807 
Sacramento, CA 95814 
Dear Ms. Millman: 
Randal Friedman 
1360 Kelton #2 
Los Angeles, CA 90024 
In response to a conversation between Mr. John Harrington and Bob 
Leidigh, wherein Senator Rosenthal requested my participation in the 
application of Mr. Murphy Dunne before the California Coastal Commission, 
I am seeking clarification of my participation pursuant to Section 83114(b) 
of the Government Code. This application has been scheduled before the 
Coastal Commission on September 29, 1983 so your prompt response would 
be appreciated. 
I was an employee of the Coastal Commission for three years. The first 
two years I was on the Permit Appeals staff in the San Francisco office, during 
which time I handled several major permits in the Malibu coastal zone. The 
last year of my employment I was assigned to the South Coast District and 
worked primarily on Malibu projects, including the Malibu Local Coastal 
Program. One of the cases I worked on was Murphy Dunne's previous application 
before the Commission, also for a single-family residence of a slightly 
different design. 
My involvement in this matter is pursuant to a request from the Senator. 
I am not under contract with Mr. Dunne, nor am I receiving any compensation 
for my work or appearance before the Commission. I have not been designated 
in any way as such on the application for a coastal development permit. 
It is my understanding based upon a conversation with Mr. Harrington 
of Senator Rosenthal's office that there does not exist any conflict of 
interest given these circumstances. It is my desire, however, to have a 
formal opinion rendered by the FPPC prior to the hearing before the Coastal 
Commission so as to not cast any doubt about this serious question. 
Again, your prompt response given the short amount of time remaining 
would be most appreciated.

11

Randal Friedman 
September 23, 1983

sections.question_synthetic: Does a former California Coastal Commission employee who worked on a permit application face a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act if asked to assist the applicant in submitting a new application for the same project, without receiving compensation?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: No conflict of interest exists under the Political Reform Act because the former employee will receive no compensation. Article 4 of Chapter 7 of the Act requires compensation as an element of the prohibition; volunteer activities are not precluded.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Complete advice letter with clear FACTS, ANALYSIS, and CONCLUSION sections. Document includes both the incoming request letter (from Friedman) and FPPC's response letter. FPPC document ID A-83-214. Dated September 23, 1983.

11 
This includes promises of future referrals of clients. 


Randal Friedman 
September 23, 1983

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel Robert Leidigh advises Randal Friedman, a former California Coastal Commission employee, that assisting an applicant on a new permit application without compensation does not create a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, since the statute's prohibitions on former employee representation require compensation.
